title: CT Head — Basic Interpretation
oneLiner: Systematic evaluation of non-contrast CT head for acute intracranial pathology; used in trauma, stroke, altered mental status; life-saving decisions depend on it.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
A 76-year-old woman on warfarin for atrial fibrillation is found on the floor by her carer. She has a GCS of 14 (E4, V4, M6), is confused, and has a left-sided facial droop. There is no obvious scalp laceration, but she is unsure if she hit her head. She is brought to the ED for a CT head. Another patient: a 22-year-old motorcyclist hit by a car, brought in with a GCS of 10 (E2, V3, M5), pupils equal and reactive, but with a expanding bruise over the right temple. A third patient: a 65-year-old man with sudden severe "thunderclap" headache and neck stiffness, GCS 15, arrives 2 hours after onset.

**Dissect the vignette clues:**
- **Warfarin + fall + confusion** → high risk of intracranial hemorrhage despite low-energy mechanism; anticoagulation amplifies any bleed.
- **Left facial droop** → suggests possible contralateral brain injury (right hemisphere); CT must rule out acute ischemic stroke or hemorrhagic stroke.
- **GCS 14 vs GCS 10** → deterioration indicates expanding mass (e.g., acute subdural) – watch for midline shift.
- **Right temple bruise** in trauma → overlies middle meningeal artery; classically epidural hematoma (lucid interval may be brief).
- **Thunderclap headache + neck stiffness** → subarachnoid hemorrhage until proven otherwise; CT sensitive within 6 hours.

**Atypical presentations:**
- Elderly with subdural hematoma may present with only confusion, no focal signs, no trauma history (spontaneous, often on anticoagulants).
- Early ischemic stroke may be missed on CT if not enough time (~6 hours) or subtle signs like loss of grey-white differentiation.
- Small subarachnoid bleeds can have normal CT if >12 hours old — lumbar puncture required.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
Non-contrast CT head is the first-line imaging for acute neurological presentations: trauma, stroke, seizure, altered mental status, headache. It detects blood (hyperdense), bone fractures, mass effect, and early signs of infarction. CT is fast, widely available, and can be performed with patients on life support or with metal implants.

**Why it matters clinically:**
- Time-sensitive decisions: thrombolysis in ischemic stroke (must exclude hemorrhage), neurosurgery for mass lesion, reversal of anticoagulation.
- Common pitfall: missing subtle signs leads to catastrophic outcomes (e.g., missing early herniation).
- Every junior doctor must be able to rapidly interpret: “Is there a surgical mass? Is there blood? Is there herniation?” before formal radiology report.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**CT density basics:** Blood appears white (hyperdense) because of iron in hemoglobin. Fresh blood (acute) is densest (40–80 HU), then gradually becomes isodense (days to weeks) and finally hypodense (old). Brain edema appears dark (hypodense). Bone is white, air is black.

**Epidural hematoma (EDH):** Usually arterial (middle meningeal artery) from skull fracture. Blood strips dura from inner table, confined by sutures (does not cross suture lines). Classic lens shape. Rapid expansion → mass effect → midline shift → herniation. Lucid interval: initial concussion, then deterioration as hematoma expands.

**Subdural hematoma (SDH):** Venous (bridging veins) from acceleration-deceleration injury (often in elderly or alcoholics with brain atrophy). Blood spreads over hemisphere, crosses sutures but not dural reflections (falx, tentorium). Crescent shape. Acute SDH hyperdense; subacute becomes isodense (can be missed if not compared to other side); chronic hypodense.

**Subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH):** Arterial rupture (most often aneurysm, especially posterior communicating, anterior communicating, middle cerebral). Blood fills cisterns and sulci. Causes vasospasm (delayed ischemic deficit). CT hyperdense blood in basal cisterns, Sylvian fissures.

**Ischemic stroke:** Cytotoxic edema → hypodensity on CT (after 6–12 hours). Early signs: loss of grey-white differentiation (e.g., insular ribbon sign), obscuration of lentiform nucleus, hyperdense middle cerebral artery sign (acute clot = hyperdense MCA). Delayed CT shows well-defined hypodensity.

**Mass effect:** Shift of midline structures (septum pellucidum, pineal gland). Uncal herniation: medial temporal lobe pushes against brainstem, compressing CN III (blown pupil, then contralateral hemiparesis). CT shows ipsilateral effacement of suprasellar cistern, contralateral hydrocephalus.

**Hydrocephalus:** Ventricles enlarge, periventricular hypodensity (transependymal edema) indicates acute obstruction. Common after SAH, posterior fossa masses.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Common Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|--------------|-----------|
| Traumatic | Falls, MVC, assault | Scalp swelling, loss of consciousness, linear skull fracture |
| Spontaneous (aneurysmal) | Berry aneurysm (MCA, ACom, PCom) | Thunderclap headache, neck stiffness, worst headache of life |
| Hypertensive | Chronic hypertension → small vessel rupture | Basal ganglia or thalamic hemorrhage; hx hypertension |
| Anticoagulant-related | Warfarin, DOACs, heparin | Any head injury (even minor) or spontaneous bleed in anticoagulated patient |
| Vascular malformation | AVM, cavernoma | Younger patient, seizures, episodic headache |
| Neoplastic | Primary (GBM) or metastatic (lung, breast, melanoma) | Gradual headache, focal deficit, known cancer |
| Inflammatory/CNS infection | Herpes encephalitis, abscess | Fever, altered consciousness, temporal lobe hypodensity |
| Venous sinus thrombosis | Hypercoagulable states, dehydration | Headache +/- seizure, hyperdense sinus sign on CT (but better on CT venogram) |

**Most common cause overall of intracranial hemorrhage:** Hypertension (followed by trauma). But in junior-level practice: always rule out traumatic first if history ambiguous.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Non-contrast CT head** is the primary test. No contrast needed for acute blood, mass effect, or large infarct. If suspect stroke, CT angiography may follow.

**Interpretation algorithm (systematic approach):**
1. **Bone window:** Look for fractures (especially over middle meningeal artery, venous sinuses). Check orbits, temporal bones, occiput.
2. **Brain window (grey-white differentiation):** Assess symmetry. Look for hyperdense (blood, calcification) or hypodense (infarct, edema) lesions.
3. **Midline shift:** Measure from septum pellucidum to midline. >5mm shift is severe; >1cm is critical.
4. **Ventricles:** Size? Effaced? Hydrocephalus? Periventricular edema?
5. **Cisterns:** Basal cisterns (suprasellar, quadrigeminal) – open or effaced? Sulci – visible or effaced?
6. **Extra-axial spaces:** Look for crescent (SDH) or lens-shaped (EDH) hyperdensity.
7. **Intra-axial:** Hemorrhage within brain (basal ganglia, lobe, posterior fossa). Hypodensities suggesting stroke.

**Pattern-recognition: single finding that rules in/out:**
- **Hyperdense MCA sign** → acute proximal MCA occlusion (ischemic stroke) – but needs CTA to confirm.
- **Lens-shaped hyperdensity over temporal lobe** → epidural hematoma – immediate neurosurgery.
- **Crescentic hyperdensity over hemisphere** → acute subdural.
- **Blood in basal cisterns** → subarachnoid hemorrhage.
- **Absence of blood on CT within 6 hours of headache** → SAH ruled out with high sensitivity (if low pretest probability) – but still need LP if high suspicion.

**Scoring: Fisher scale for SAH** (helps predict vasospasm risk). **Marshall CT classification for traumatic brain injury** (used by neurosurgeons, but not expected at FY1). Know **Rotterdam CT score** (predicts mortality after TBI) – includes midline shift, effacement of basal cisterns, epidural/supratentorial lesion.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
Management is not about reading the CT yourself in isolation – it's about acting on findings. The key question: **Is there a surgical mass?** If yes, call neurosurgery immediately.

**Immediate (first hour):**
- If CT shows **epidural hematoma (EDH)** with mass effect: immediate neurosurgical evacuation. Do NOT intubate unless GCS dropping – hyperventilation can cause vasoconstriction but is temporizing. Give mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg IV (not in hypotensive) or hypertonic saline.
- If **acute subdural (SDH)** with midline shift >5mm or GCS<13: same urgency. Reverse anticoagulation urgently: warfarin → vitamin K 10mg IV + PCC (prothrombin complex concentrate) 25–50 U/kg. DOAC → idarucizumab (dabigatran) or andexanet alfa (apixaban, rivaroxaban) if available.
- If **subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH):** strict systolic BP <140 mmHg (use nimodipine 60mg PO/NG 4-hourly for 21 days to prevent vasospasm). Contact neurosurgery for aneurysm coiling/clipping.
- If **ischemic stroke** (no hemorrhage): consider thrombolysis if within 4.5 hours of onset + no contraindications (alteplase 0.9 mg/kg IV, max 90mg; 10% bolus, rest over 1 hour). CT must show no bleed and no extensive infarct (ASPECTS score >7).
- If **intraparenchymal hemorrhage (IPH):** BP control (SBP <140 mmHg; use IV labetalol or nicardipine). Reverse anticoagulation as above. Consider neurosurgery if cerebellar >3cm, lobar with mass effect, or deteriorating.

**Ward (24–72h):**
- Repeat CT if deterioration (drop GCS by 2, new pupil abnormality, limb weakness).
- Monitor for vasospasm after SAH (daily TCD, clinical vigilance).
- Control ICP (head of bed 30°, sedation, mannitol, hyperventilation only on vent as bridge).
- Antiepileptics not routine in IPH without seizure.

**Long-term:**
- Rehabilitation, seizure prophylaxis if brain injury, BP control for hypertension, aneurysm treatment follow-up.
- Avoid NSAIDs in any intracranial hemorrhage.

**Common pitfalls:**
- Do not give thrombolysis if CT shows any blood – even a small focal bleed (hemorrhagic conversion of ischemic stroke is later, but acute bleed = contraindication).
- Do not delay CT in head injury if on warfarin/DOAC even if GCS 15 and no symptoms – they can deteriorate hours later.
- Do not forget to check for hyperdense MCA sign – even if no symptoms – indicates large vessel occlusion requiring thrombectomy.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Epidural Hematoma (EDH) | Subdural Hematoma (SDH) | Subarachnoid Hemorrhage (SAH) | Intraparenchymal Hemorrhage (IPH) |
|---------|--------------------------|--------------------------|--------------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Shape** | Biconvex (lens) | Crescent | Diffuse (cisterns, sulci) | Round/irregular within brain |
| **Crosses sutures** | No (limited by sutures) | Yes (not limited) | N/A (in subarachnoid space) | N/A |
| **Crosses dural reflections** | No (limited by falx/tentorium) | Yes (crescent crosses midline only if isodense and extends around) | Yes (blood in cisterns) | No (within brain tissue) |
| **Classic history** | Trauma (often temporoparietal) | Trauma (elderly, alcoholics, falls) | Thunderclap headache | Hypertension, amyloid angiopathy |
| **Lucid interval** | Common | Rare (more gradual) | Not typical | Not typical |
| **Age group** | Young (MVC), falls | Elderly, anticoagulated | 30–60 (aneurysm) | Elderly, hypertensive |
| **CT density** | Hyperdense (acute) | Variable: hyper acute → hypodense chronic | Hyperdense in cisterns | Hyperdense (acute) then hypodense over days-weeks |
| **Key differentiator** | Lens shape, does not cross sutures, middle meningeal artery | Crescent shape, crosses sutures, associated with brain atrophy | Blood in basal cisterns | Location (basal ganglia, thalamus, cerebellum, lobar) |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
- **Brain herniation (transtentorial, subfalcine, tonsillar):** CT shows midline shift >5mm, effacement of basal cisterns, ipsilateral ventricle compression. Signs: blown pupil (ipsilateral CN III), contralateral hemiparesis (then bilateral), Cushing's reflex (bradycardia, hypertension, irregular breathing). Action: immediate hyperventilation, mannitol, neurosurgery.
- **Seizures** (early post-traumatic or after IPH): risk is highest within first 24 hours. Not routinely prophylactic (except in penetrating TBI or large contusions). If occurs: levetiracetam 500–1500 mg IV bid.
- **Vasospasm after SAH** (3–14 days post-hemorrhage): new focal deficit, confusion, drop in consciousness. CT may show new infarction. TCD shows elevated velocities (>200 cm/s). Treatment: triple-H therapy (hemodilution, hypertension, hypervolemia) is now less favored – nimodipine, angioplasty, intra-arterial verapamil.
- **Hydrocephalus after SAH or posterior fossa bleed:** headache, vomiting, drowsiness. CT shows ventricular dilation with periventricular edema. External ventricular drain (EVD) may be needed.
- **Rebleeding** (most common in SAH within first 6 hours if not treated): sudden deterioration. CT shows new blood. Action: emergency angiography, coiling.
- **Hemorrhagic conversion of ischemic stroke:** usually within 24–48 hours after thrombolysis or large infarct. CT shows new petechial or frank hematoma. Stop thrombolysis/antiplatelets, manage as IPH.

**Late presentation pattern:** A patient with chronic subdural hematoma may present weeks after minor fall with confusion, gait disturbance, headache (especially in elderly). CT shows isodense crescent – often bilateral, can mimic stroke or dementia.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonic for systematic CT head review:** “**B**lood **C**an **B**e **V**ery **B**ad” – but more helpful: **A-B-C-D-E**
- **A** – Asymmetry (midline shift, ventricles, cisterns)
- **B** – Blood (hyperdense lesions: epidural, subdural, subarachnoid, intraparenchymal)
- **C** – Cisterns (basal, suprasellar, quadrigeminal: effaced? = mass effect)
- **D** – Density (grey-white differentiation, hypodensities: stroke, edema)
- **E** – Extra-axial spaces (epidural, subdural, subarachnoid)

**Mnemonic for management of acute ICH:** “**H**ypotension (avoid), **H**emia (surgery), **H**ematoma expansion (BP control, reversal), **H**ydrocephalus (EVD)”

**Pearls:**
1. Always compare left to right – subtle asymmetry is the earliest sign of pathology.
2. The hyperdense MCA sign can be mistaken for dense calcification – check if it appears as a “dot” in the Sylvian fissure.
3. A small subdural may only be visible on the most superior slices (near the vertex) where it looks like a thin crescent hugging the inner table.
4. Basal ganglia hemorrhage in a young patient (no hypertension) raises suspicion for underlying arteriovenous malformation – needs CTA.
5. After thrombolysis for ischemic stroke, a repeat CT is done if new headache/vomiting/neurologic decline – look for hemorrhagic transformation.
6. If CT shows no bleed but patient has high suspicion for SAH (thunderclap headache), still do LP (xanthochromia at 12 hours).
7. In trauma, always look at the bone window for subtle skull fractures – could indicate open skull fracture if overlying air or scalp swelling.

**Common exam/ward mistake:**
- Calling a normal artifact (e.g., beam hardening from bone) as blood – check all window levels and compare to opposite side.
- Missing a small, isodense subacute SDH in an elderly patient with brain atrophy – the crescent may be subtle; look for loss of sulci over that hemisphere.
- Forgetting to check for hydrocephalus after SAH – basal cistern effacement may be from blood alone, but ventricular dilation indicates outflow obstruction.
- Assuming a small IPH is benign – even 10 mL of blood in the thalamus can cause significant mass effect.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior (registrar/consultant) immediately:**
- GCS drop by ≥2 points (any cause)
- New pupillary asymmetry (dilated, unreactive) – suggests uncal herniation
- New focal motor deficit
- Seizure (first in this admission)
- Findings on CT that require neurosurgical input:
    - **Midline shift >5mm**
    - **Epidural hematoma (any size with symptoms)**
    - **Subdural hematoma with thickness >10mm or shift >5mm**
    - **Posterior fossa hemorrhage (cerebellar/brainstem) – any size because of limited space**
    - **Hydrocephalus** (especially if acute with periventricular edema)
    - **Mass effect causing cisternal effacement**
    - **Open skull fracture (with pneumocephalus)**

**When to call ICU / crash team:**
- GCS ≤8 (or rapid drop) – need airway protection
- Ongoing convulsions despite IV lorazepam (status epilepticus)
- Cushing's reflex (bradycardia, hypertension, irregular breathing) – imminent herniation
- NEWS2 ≥7 (or any sustained SpO2 <90% despite O2, RR <8 or >30, HR <40 or >140, SBP <90)
- Lactate >4 (if available) – may indicate herniation-induced autonomic instability

**Specific thresholds:**
- **GCS ≤8** → intubate (protect airway, maintain PaCO2 ~35 mmHg)
- **Midline shift >5mm** → call neurosurgeon; if >10mm and comatose, proceed to OR without delay
- **Systolic BP >200 mmHg** in acute ICH → burst reduction to <180 (then to <140) over 15–20 min using IV labetalol/nicardipine
- **Hypotension (MAP <70)** → rare in isolated neuro trauma but may indicate polytrauma; need volume + norepinephrine
- **Hyperthermia >38°C** → worsens neurologic outcome; treat aggressively with paracetamol and cooling

**Remember:** The CT is a single snapshot. Clinical assessment (GCS, pupils, motor exam) is dynamic and equally important. If the patient's clinical status is worse than the CT suggests, escalate – the next CT may already be hours away.

# CT Head — Basic Interpretation

**One-liner:** Systematic evaluation of non-contrast CT head for acute intracranial pathology; used in trauma, stroke, altered mental status; life-saving decisions depend on it.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

A 76-year-old woman on warfarin for atrial fibrillation is found on the floor by her carer. She has a GCS of 14 (E4, V4, M6), is confused, and has a left-sided facial droop. There is no obvious scalp laceration, but she is unsure if she hit her head. She is brought to the ED for a CT head. Another patient: a 22-year-old motorcyclist hit by a car, brought in with a GCS of 10 (E2, V3, M5), pupils equal and reactive, but with a expanding bruise over the right temple. A third patient: a 65-year-old man with sudden severe "thunderclap" headache and neck stiffness, GCS 15, arrives 2 hours after onset.

**Dissect the vignette clues:**
- **Warfarin + fall + confusion** → high risk of intracranial hemorrhage despite low-energy mechanism; anticoagulation amplifies any bleed.
- **Left facial droop** → suggests possible contralateral brain injury (right hemisphere); CT must rule out acute ischemic stroke or hemorrhagic stroke.
- **GCS 14 vs GCS 10** → deterioration indicates expanding mass (e.g., acute subdural) – watch for midline shift.
- **Right temple bruise** in trauma → overlies middle meningeal artery; classically epidural hematoma (lucid interval may be brief).
- **Thunderclap headache + neck stiffness** → subarachnoid hemorrhage until proven otherwise; CT sensitive within 6 hours.

**Atypical presentations:**
- Elderly with subdural hematoma may present with only confusion, no focal signs, no trauma history (spontaneous, often on anticoagulants).
- Early ischemic stroke may be missed on CT if not enough time (~6 hours) or subtle signs like loss of grey-white differentiation.
- Small subarachnoid bleeds can have normal CT if >12 hours old — lumbar puncture required.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

Non-contrast CT head is the first-line imaging for acute neurological presentations: trauma, stroke, seizure, altered mental status, headache. It detects blood (hyperdense), bone fractures, mass effect, and early signs of infarction. CT is fast, widely available, and can be performed with patients on life support or with metal implants.

**Why it matters clinically:**
- Time-sensitive decisions: thrombolysis in ischemic stroke (must exclude hemorrhage), neurosurgery for mass lesion, reversal of anticoagulation.
- Common pitfall: missing subtle signs leads to catastrophic outcomes (e.g., missing early herniation).
- Every junior doctor must be able to rapidly interpret: “Is there a surgical mass? Is there blood? Is there herniation?” before formal radiology report.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**CT density basics:** Blood appears white (hyperdense) because of iron in hemoglobin. Fresh blood (acute) is densest (40–80 HU), then gradually becomes isodense (days to weeks) and finally hypodense (old). Brain edema appears dark (hypodense). Bone is white, air is black.

**Epidural hematoma (EDH):** Usually arterial (middle meningeal artery) from skull fracture. Blood strips dura from inner table, confined by sutures (does not cross suture lines). Classic lens shape. Rapid expansion → mass effect → midline shift → herniation. Lucid interval: initial concussion, then deterioration as hematoma expands.

**Subdural hematoma (SDH):** Venous (bridging veins) from acceleration-deceleration injury (often in elderly or alcoholics with brain atrophy). Blood spreads over hemisphere, crosses sutures but not dural reflections (falx, tentorium). Crescent shape. Acute SDH hyperdense; subacute becomes isodense (can be missed if not compared to other side); chronic hypodense.

**Subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH):** Arterial rupture (most often aneurysm, especially posterior communicating, anterior communicating, middle cerebral). Blood fills cisterns and sulci. Causes vasospasm (delayed ischemic deficit). CT hyperdense blood in basal cisterns, Sylvian fissures.

**Ischemic stroke:** Cytotoxic edema → hypodensity on CT (after 6–12 hours). Early signs: loss of grey-white differentiation (e.g., insular ribbon sign), obscuration of lentiform nucleus, hyperdense middle cerebral artery sign (acute clot = hyperdense MCA). Delayed CT shows well-defined hypodensity.

**Mass effect:** Shift of midline structures (septum pellucidum, pineal gland). Uncal herniation: medial temporal lobe pushes against brainstem, compressing CN III (blown pupil, then contralateral hemiparesis). CT shows ipsilateral effacement of suprasellar cistern, contralateral hydrocephalus.

**Hydrocephalus:** Ventricles enlarge, periventricular hypodensity (transependymal edema) indicates acute obstruction. Common after SAH, posterior fossa masses.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Common Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|--------------|-----------|
| Traumatic | Falls, MVC, assault | Scalp swelling, loss of consciousness, linear skull fracture |
| Spontaneous (aneurysmal) | Berry aneurysm (MCA, ACom, PCom) | Thunderclap headache, neck stiffness, worst headache of life |
| Hypertensive | Chronic hypertension → small vessel rupture | Basal ganglia or thalamic hemorrhage; hx hypertension |
| Anticoagulant-related | Warfarin, DOACs, heparin | Any head injury (even minor) or spontaneous bleed in anticoagulated patient |
| Vascular malformation | AVM, cavernoma | Younger patient, seizures, episodic headache |
| Neoplastic | Primary (GBM) or metastatic (lung, breast, melanoma) | Gradual headache, focal deficit, known cancer |
| Inflammatory/CNS infection | Herpes encephalitis, abscess | Fever, altered consciousness, temporal lobe hypodensity |
| Venous sinus thrombosis | Hypercoagulable states, dehydration | Headache +/- seizure, hyperdense sinus sign on CT (but better on CT venogram) |

**Most common cause overall of intracranial hemorrhage:** Hypertension (followed by trauma). But in junior-level practice: always rule out traumatic first if history ambiguous.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Non-contrast CT head** is the primary test. No contrast needed for acute blood, mass effect, or large infarct. If suspect stroke, CT angiography may follow.

**Interpretation algorithm (systematic approach):**
1. **Bone window:** Look for fractures (especially over middle meningeal artery, venous sinuses). Check orbits, temporal bones, occiput.
2. **Brain window (grey-white differentiation):** Assess symmetry. Look for hyperdense (blood, calcification) or hypodense (infarct, edema) lesions.
3. **Midline shift:** Measure from septum pellucidum to midline. >5mm shift is severe; >1cm is critical.
4. **Ventricles:** Size? Effaced? Hydrocephalus? Periventricular edema?
5. **Cisterns:** Basal cisterns (suprasellar, quadrigeminal) – open or effaced? Sulci – visible or effaced?
6. **Extra-axial spaces:** Look for crescent (SDH) or lens-shaped (EDH) hyperdensity.
7. **Intra-axial:** Hemorrhage within brain (basal ganglia, lobe, posterior fossa). Hypodensities suggesting stroke.

**Pattern-recognition: single finding that rules in/out:**
- **Hyperdense MCA sign** → acute proximal MCA occlusion (ischemic stroke) – but needs CTA to confirm.
- **Lens-shaped hyperdensity over temporal lobe** → epidural hematoma – immediate neurosurgery.
- **Crescentic hyperdensity over hemisphere** → acute subdural.
- **Blood in basal cisterns** → subarachnoid hemorrhage.
- **Absence of blood on CT within 6 hours of headache** → SAH ruled out with high sensitivity (if low pretest probability) – but still need LP if high suspicion.

**Scoring: Fisher scale for SAH** (helps predict vasospasm risk). **Marshall CT classification for traumatic brain injury** (used by neurosurgeons, but not expected at FY1). Know **Rotterdam CT score** (predicts mortality after TBI) – includes midline shift, effacement of basal cisterns, epidural/supratentorial lesion.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

Management is not about reading the CT yourself in isolation – it's about acting on findings. The key question: **Is there a surgical mass?** If yes, call neurosurgery immediately.

**Immediate (first hour):**
- If CT shows **epidural hematoma (EDH)** with mass effect: immediate neurosurgical evacuation. Do NOT intubate unless GCS dropping – hyperventilation can cause vasoconstriction but is temporizing. Give mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg IV (not in hypotensive) or hypertonic saline.
- If **acute subdural (SDH)** with midline shift >5mm or GCS<13: same urgency. Reverse anticoagulation urgently: warfarin → vitamin K 10mg IV + PCC (prothrombin complex concentrate) 25–50 U/kg. DOAC → idarucizumab (dabigatran) or andexanet alfa (apixaban, rivaroxaban) if available.
- If **subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH):** strict systolic BP <140 mmHg (use nimodipine 60mg PO/NG 4-hourly for 21 days to prevent vasospasm). Contact neurosurgery for aneurysm coiling/clipping.
- If **ischemic stroke** (no hemorrhage): consider thrombolysis if within 4.5 hours of onset + no contraindications (alteplase 0.9 mg/kg IV, max 90mg; 10% bolus, rest over 1 hour). CT must show no bleed and no extensive infarct (ASPECTS score >7).
- If **intraparenchymal hemorrhage (IPH):** BP control (SBP <140 mmHg; use IV labetalol or nicardipine). Reverse anticoagulation as above. Consider neurosurgery if cerebellar >3cm, lobar with mass effect, or deteriorating.

**Ward (24–72h):**
- Repeat CT if deterioration (drop GCS by 2, new pupil abnormality, limb weakness).
- Monitor for vasospasm after SAH (daily TCD, clinical vigilance).
- Control ICP (head of bed 30°, sedation, mannitol, hyperventilation only on vent as bridge).
- Antiepileptics not routine in IPH without seizure.

**Long-term:**
- Rehabilitation, seizure prophylaxis if brain injury, BP control for hypertension, aneurysm treatment follow-up.
- Avoid NSAIDs in any intracranial hemorrhage.

**Common pitfalls:**
- Do not give thrombolysis if CT shows any blood – even a small focal bleed (hemorrhagic conversion of ischemic stroke is later, but acute bleed = contraindication).
- Do not delay CT in head injury if on warfarin/DOAC even if GCS 15 and no symptoms – they can deteriorate hours later.
- Do not forget to check for hyperdense MCA sign – even if no symptoms – indicates large vessel occlusion requiring thrombectomy.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Epidural Hematoma (EDH) | Subdural Hematoma (SDH) | Subarachnoid Hemorrhage (SAH) | Intraparenchymal Hemorrhage (IPH) |
|---------|--------------------------|--------------------------|--------------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Shape** | Biconvex (lens) | Crescent | Diffuse (cisterns, sulci) | Round/irregular within brain |
| **Crosses sutures** | No (limited by sutures) | Yes (not limited) | N/A (in subarachnoid space) | N/A |
| **Crosses dural reflections** | No (limited by falx/tentorium) | Yes (crescent crosses midline only if isodense and extends around) | Yes (blood in cisterns) | No (within brain tissue) |
| **Classic history** | Trauma (often temporoparietal) | Trauma (elderly, alcoholics, falls) | Thunderclap headache | Hypertension, amyloid angiopathy |
| **Lucid interval** | Common | Rare (more gradual) | Not typical | Not typical |
| **Age group** | Young (MVC), falls | Elderly, anticoagulated | 30–60 (aneurysm) | Elderly, hypertensive |
| **CT density** | Hyperdense (acute) | Variable: hyper acute → hypodense chronic | Hyperdense in cisterns | Hyperdense (acute) then hypodense over days-weeks |
| **Key differentiator** | Lens shape, does not cross sutures, middle meningeal artery | Crescent shape, crosses sutures, associated with brain atrophy | Blood in basal cisterns | Location (basal ganglia, thalamus, cerebellum, lobar) |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

- **Brain herniation (transtentorial, subfalcine, tonsillar):** CT shows midline shift >5mm, effacement of basal cisterns, ipsilateral ventricle compression. Signs: blown pupil (ipsilateral CN III), contralateral hemiparesis (then bilateral), Cushing's reflex (bradycardia, hypertension, irregular breathing). Action: immediate hyperventilation, mannitol, neurosurgery.
- **Seizures** (early post-traumatic or after IPH): risk is highest within first 24 hours. Not routinely prophylactic (except in penetrating TBI or large contusions). If occurs: levetiracetam 500–1500 mg IV bid.
- **Vasospasm after SAH** (3–14 days post-hemorrhage): new focal deficit, confusion, drop in consciousness. CT may show new infarction. TCD shows elevated velocities (>200 cm/s). Treatment: triple-H therapy (hemodilution, hypertension, hypervolemia) is now less favored – nimodipine, angioplasty, intra-arterial verapamil.
- **Hydrocephalus after SAH or posterior fossa bleed:** headache, vomiting, drowsiness. CT shows ventricular dilation with periventricular edema. External ventricular drain (EVD) may be needed.
- **Rebleeding** (most common in SAH within first 6 hours if not treated): sudden deterioration. CT shows new blood. Action: emergency angiography, coiling.
- **Hemorrhagic conversion of ischemic stroke:** usually within 24–48 hours after thrombolysis or large infarct. CT shows new petechial or frank hematoma. Stop thrombolysis/antiplatelets, manage as IPH.

**Late presentation pattern:** A patient with chronic subdural hematoma may present weeks after minor fall with confusion, gait disturbance, headache (especially in elderly). CT shows isodense crescent – often bilateral, can mimic stroke or dementia.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonic for systematic CT head review:** “**B**lood **C**an **B**e **V**ery **B**ad” – but more helpful: **A-B-C-D-E**
- **A** – Asymmetry (midline shift, ventricles, cisterns)
- **B** – Blood (hyperdense lesions: epidural, subdural, subarachnoid, intraparenchymal)
- **C** – Cisterns (basal, suprasellar, quadrigeminal: effaced? = mass effect)
- **D** – Density (grey-white differentiation, hypodensities: stroke, edema)
- **E** – Extra-axial spaces (epidural, subdural, subarachnoid)

**Mnemonic for management of acute ICH:** “**H**ypotension (avoid), **H**emia (surgery), **H**ematoma expansion (BP control, reversal), **H**ydrocephalus (EVD)”

**Pearls:**
1. Always compare left to right – subtle asymmetry is the earliest sign of pathology.
2. The hyperdense MCA sign can be mistaken for dense calcification – check if it appears as a “dot” in the Sylvian fissure.
3. A small subdural may only be visible on the most superior slices (near the vertex) where it looks like a thin crescent hugging the inner table.
4. Basal ganglia hemorrhage in a young patient (no hypertension) raises suspicion for underlying arteriovenous malformation – needs CTA.
5. After thrombolysis for ischemic stroke, a repeat CT is done if new headache/vomiting/neurologic decline – look for hemorrhagic transformation.
6. If CT shows no bleed but patient has high suspicion for SAH (thunderclap headache), still do LP (xanthochromia at 12 hours).
7. In trauma, always look at the bone window for subtle skull fractures – could indicate open skull fracture if overlying air or scalp swelling.

**Common exam/ward mistake:**
- Calling a normal artifact (e.g., beam hardening from bone) as blood – check all window levels and compare to opposite side.
- Missing a small, isodense subacute SDH in an elderly patient with brain atrophy – the crescent may be subtle; look for loss of sulci over that hemisphere.
- Forgetting to check for hydrocephalus after SAH – basal cistern effacement may be from blood alone, but ventricular dilation indicates outflow obstruction.
- Assuming a small IPH is benign – even 10 mL of blood in the thalamus can cause significant mass effect.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call senior (registrar/consultant) immediately:**
- GCS drop by ≥2 points (any cause)
- New pupillary asymmetry (dilated, unreactive) – suggests uncal herniation
- New focal motor deficit
- Seizure (first in this admission)
- Findings on CT that require neurosurgical input:
    - **Midline shift >5mm**
    - **Epidural hematoma (any size with symptoms)**
    - **Subdural hematoma with thickness >10mm or shift >5mm**
    - **Posterior fossa hemorrhage (cerebellar/brainstem) – any size because of limited space**
    - **Hydrocephalus** (especially if acute with periventricular edema)
    - **Mass effect causing cisternal effacement**
    - **Open skull fracture (with pneumocephalus)**

**When to call ICU / crash team:**
- GCS ≤8 (or rapid drop) – need airway protection
- Ongoing convulsions despite IV lorazepam (status epilepticus)
- Cushing's reflex (bradycardia, hypertension, irregular breathing) – imminent herniation
- NEWS2 ≥7 (or any sustained SpO2 <90% despite O2, RR <8 or >30, HR <40 or >140, SBP <90)
- Lactate >4 (if available) – may indicate herniation-induced autonomic instability

**Specific thresholds:**
- **GCS ≤8** → intubate (protect airway, maintain PaCO2 ~35 mmHg)
- **Midline shift >5mm** → call neurosurgeon; if >10mm and comatose, proceed to OR without delay
- **Systolic BP >200 mmHg** in acute ICH → burst reduction to <180 (then to <140) over 15–20 min using IV labetalol/nicardipine
- **Hypotension (MAP <70)** → rare in isolated neuro trauma but may indicate polytrauma; need volume + norepinephrine
- **Hyperthermia >38°C** → worsens neurologic outcome; treat aggressively with paracetamol and cooling

**Remember:** The CT is a single snapshot. Clinical assessment (GCS, pupils, motor exam) is dynamic and equally important. If the patient's clinical status is worse than the CT suggests, escalate – the next CT may already be hours away.
